item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image appears to be from here, which is All Rights Reserved. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way too many "cases before being contained". It just becomes stupid halfway through; this thing has been causing all kinds of mayhem for decades and the Foundation didn't notice or relate the cases in any way. They seem comically inept, and I'm guessing you didn't intend that to happen.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read this:

and began containment with aerosol dispersal of Amnesiacs.

I see this:

and began containment with aerosol dispersal of people who forgot who they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but you're pushing suspension of disbelief on the "whoops, missed this one five times over eighty years".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this, but I seem to like the dense wall of text SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sort of reminds me of the emotion-absorbing yeast, in a good way though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even most of the the relatively severe effects wouldn't prevent someone from line-editing the MS. And the process of fixing typos and punctuation would likely de-SCPfy the manuscript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was considering that, but it strikes me that it could go south pretty quickly. It also sort of commits me to keep an eye on the experiment logs when I do have a habit of disappearing for moths at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty neat and well-detailed, and it tells a few stories along the way. A good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm cautious because it's an SCP that makes SCPs, since they all do different things.

This is a big chunk of my problem with the article. The only connection between the things it puts out is "they're all text"; the types of anomalies we're seeing are too scattered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While quite long, I think it shows a great deal of diversity, similar to 914 in some respects. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hit the last document and became overwhelmed by the footnotes.

That said, I wasn't too impressed by this. I laughed at the fanfic test, but the notes for it and the one following were pretty lolfoundation. The diary test was the only thing that really caught me. I'm cautious because it's an SCP that makes SCPs, since they all do different things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
██ of these stories had seen publication, but only SCP-1804-58A had made it into print without editing, and retained SCP-1804's effect

Something bugs me about this. Wouldn't the whole compulsive effect interfere with the editing process?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Would you consider moving the testing logs to a separate article and making them collaborative, 914-style?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was genius. Both in inspiration and execution. Well done!
I've found myself re-reading it.

I like extensive footnotes. Read them if you want is my motto. I don't understand why others complain about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the idea of this article. The length doesn't bother me… the only gripe I have is that it gets a case of the [REDACTED]s near the end, but that seems easily fixed. Always good to see someone put a substantial amount of effort into their skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I imagine you'll get a bit of that.

Which isn't fair; both to the author and to the work itself. To be fair, the article's not inherently a bad thing; it's just really not my kind of thing. I just felt that I was going to get a big horrific payoff at the end of this, and I didn't and was overly (most likely wrongly) disappointed.

I do want to see a little more variety in the tone of different authors. Something like this shouldn't be attempted by first-time authors, but I really want to be able to read an article and then shout "That's so [REDACTED]!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More or less agreeing with Hyenaboy; I sort of assume that each SCP has its own experiment file, in which things like the exact poem typed up using SCP-1804, or the original designations of works associated with it, are listed. My understanding was that the point of an SCP file is to provide what information is relevant to containing the object, not to list everything the Foundation knows about the object. But you [sandrewswann] have been around considerably longer than I have, so you're probably in a better position to say whether that's actually the case.

I'm fairly neutral on the concept itself; you've supported it well, and it's an effect that seems logical, given the physical form of the SCP, but there's nothing about it that's particularly surprising after the first paragraph of the description. ("Turns out that was not a great idea" made me chuckle a little.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I probably do. I have a habit of pushing the form as far as I can, and sometimes *cough*"kid who made it impossible for someone to write about him"*cough* I can go too far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I get some time later today I'll do a pass to see about removing some of the excess footnotes and redactions.

EDIT: I did some un-footnoting and un-redacting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda liked the pedantry of it. Even in-universe, different people compose these documents, and some would logically be a bit more verbose than others, or make greater use of footnotes. I slogged through the whole of it, liked it, and upvoted it. In all fairness, Swann, I might not have done that for an SCP if I hadn't had some previous faith in the author's writing ability. Keep in mind that nobody's obligated to do anything more than see the wall of text, decide it's not worth it, and downvote. I imagine you'll get a bit of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely fucking nuts. Upvoted for that reason.

Now, as for redaction, I think my feelings on the subject are pretty clear at this point. This really, really skirts the line for redactions, in that there are approximately eight zillion of them. But they're all used pretty well, until the last addendum. That's where I started seeing things like:

Two days after the publication of SCP-1804-8A, a riot occurred in which the "Bucking Bronco" saloon was burned to the ground and a mob [REDACTED] City Hall. Foundation agents [REDACTED] aerosol dispersal of Amnesiacs [REDACTED] shootout between Mr. D██████ and MTF agents [REDACTED] recovered all copies of SCP-1804-8A in the aftermath.

Which I, of course, read as

Two days after the publication of SCP-1804-8A, a riot occured in which the "Bucking Bronco" saloon was burned to the ground and a mob built an enormous stack of blueberry pancakes in the shape of City Hall. Foundation agents consumed the pancakes and thanked their producers, almost forgetting why they had come in the first place. In the Foundation Scholarly Press article referencing the incident, "Pancake Days: Study of the aerosol dispersal of Amnesiacs in enclosed locations" (Eskobar, 1921), it was theorized that the pancakes contained an unknown hallucinogenic agent that caused the engaging agents to lose track of their intended goal. Regardless, a follow-up investigation by MTF Sigma-Six ("Unnecessarily Long Redactors") returned to the site, engaging in the perfunctory shootout between Mr. Dyne and MTF agents that happens in all of these incidents. An alchemist recovered all copies of SCP-1804-8A in the aftermath.

In short, you might deredact a bit. It looks a little silly there towards the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Holy pedantic footnotes Batman!"

I can see that you put a lot of work into this one, and technically speaking there are few articles that rival it. However this article suffers from some failings that really bring it down.

The footnotes: Way too many. I feel like I'm reading the idiot's version of Paradise Lost again. Aside from the one (whose existence is dubious at this moment) kid who made it impossible for someone to write about him, I have never seen so many footnotes. In this case, is starts to border on being a bad thing, and it becomes more of a chore to read them; most of the time they barely add to the text. The [REDACTED] you used is okay in small quantities, and I think you used it well, but then you started in with way too much of it towards the end.

Also, the concept. It doesn't really do anything for me. In summary, it's "typewriter what types things what do some odd thing to people when they read it". It really doesn't pique my curiosity, as the effects are often mild and while there are some exciting ones, they are bogged down and weakened by the sheer volume of text which tends to err on the boring side.

You have good stuff in there somewhere, but it is buried under dull text and technical (also dull) writing. If you clean it up and put more of an edge on it, I would love to upvote it, but right now it feels like a mess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my head, this (or something like it) is what SCP-1127 was scripted on.
I hope I got across the things I think are scary about this, predominantly the fact that it was uncontained for something like 80 years, and most of the effects are too subtle to get on the Foundation's radar.
